text: When designing a REST API, following best practices ensures it is easy to use and maintain. Endpoints should be named using nouns, not verbs, and represent resources clearly, like `/users` or `/orders`. Use standard HTTP status codes to indicate results: `200` for success, `201` for created resources, `400` for bad requests, and `404` for not found. Good documentation is crucial—it helps developers understand how to interact with your API. Finally, always implement versioning, such as `/v1/users`, so that changes don’t break existing applications. By keeping these principles in mind, your API will be reliable and developer-friendly.
